summary: Explore patient-led spiritual care for people of every faith, worldview and none.
objectives: Describe chaplaincy as support for religious, spiritual and existential needs.
Keep the patient’s beliefs and values at the centre of the encounter.
Know when to offer a referral to a chaplain or faith representative.

sections.title: What might matter today?
body: Illness can disturb a person’s sense of meaning, identity, hope, belonging or connection. Some people draw on a religious faith. Others draw on relationships, nature, culture, memories, values or the life they have lived.
Chaplaincy begins by discovering what matters to this person, at this moment. It does not begin by assuming what they believe.

items.title: Feel less alone
items.body: Hospital can be isolating. An unhurried conversation can restore a sense of human connection.

items.title: Express difficult feelings
items.body: Fear, anger, grief, uncertainty and questions such as “why me?” may need somewhere safe to be spoken.

items.title: Draw on what sustains them
items.body: This might involve relationships, beliefs, memories, prayer, ritual, meaning, identity or hope.

items.title: Reach appropriate support
items.body: A volunteer can refer requests for prayer, sacrament, rites or specialist support to the chaplaincy team.

do.title: Do
items: Use the words the patient uses for their beliefs and experience.
Offer an appropriate referral when requested.

dont.title: Avoid
items: Assuming that a person is religious or non-religious.
Trying to persuade, convert or correct someone.
Giving confident answers to profound questions.
Promising an outcome from prayer or belief.

sections.label: Try this
sections.title: “I’m not religious”
sections.prompt: You introduce yourself as a chaplaincy volunteer. The patient replies, “Oh, I’m not religious.” What is the most helpful response?

options.text: “That’s absolutely fine. We visit people of all beliefs and none. Would you still like a chat?”
options.feedback: This corrects the misunderstanding without pressure and gives the patient a genuine choice.

options.text: “You might feel differently when you have thought about what is happening to you.”
options.feedback: This dismisses the patient’s stated position and risks making chaplaincy feel coercive.
Leaving may be appropriate if that is what the patient wants, but first clarify that chaplaincy is available to people who are not religious.

sections.debrief: The aim is neither to sell chaplaincy nor to withdraw it automatically. Explain briefly, offer choice and respect the answer.
